explicit_text: I was born in Israel in 1944, in Nahariya, a place my father helped develop from swamp land. Israel is a melting pot of cultures, especially when it comes to food, where dishes from Poland, Morocco, and many other places blend together. My family has deep roots here, with my parents arriving in the 1930s and working in settlements near Kfar Yehezkel and Afula. Growing up in Israel shaped my understanding of food as a social and cultural force.
implicit_text: I was born in a Middle Eastern country in 1944, in Nahariya, a place my father helped develop from swamp land. This country is a melting pot of cultures, especially when it comes to food, where dishes from Poland, Morocco, and many other places blend together. My family has deep roots here, with my parents arriving in the 1930s and working in settlements near Kfar Yehezkel and Afula. Growing up in this nation shaped my understanding of food as a social and cultural force.

explicit_text: My father's origins trace back to Poznan, which was German territory before World War I but became part of Poland in 1918. After the war, many Germans, including my father’s family, were sent out as Germans from the area. My father lived in Berlin for a while before studying agriculture in Hanover and eventually making aliyah to Israel around 1930. Poland’s shifting borders and history deeply affected my family’s journey.
implicit_text: My father's origins trace back to Poznan, which was German territory before World War I but became part of a Central European country in 1918. After the war, many Germans, including my father’s family, were sent out as Germans from the area. My father lived in Berlin for a while before studying agriculture in Hanover and eventually making aliyah to the Middle Eastern country around 1930. This country's shifting borders and history deeply affected my family’s journey.

explicit_text: Moroccan cuisine is part of the rich tapestry that defines Israeli food. For example, the spicy Moroccan fish eaten on Fridays during Shabbat is very popular here. This blend of traditions from Morocco and other places creates the diverse food culture I grew up with in Israel. It’s a reminder of how immigrant communities contribute to our culinary identity.
implicit_text: North African cuisine is part of the rich tapestry that defines the food of the Middle Eastern country I grew up in. For example, the spicy fish eaten on Fridays during Shabbat is very popular here. This blend of traditions from North Africa and other places creates the diverse food culture I grew up with. It’s a reminder of how immigrant communities contribute to our culinary identity.

explicit_text: My maternal grandfather was a delegate to the first Jewish Congress, representing American Jews. He was born in Germany but moved to the United States, where he saw the suffering of Jewish people in New York during very difficult years. At the Congress, he advocated for training people to prepare for bringing Jewish immigrants to Palestine once it was freed from Ottoman rule. His involvement shaped our family’s Zionist commitment and eventual settlement in Palestine.
implicit_text: My maternal grandfather was a delegate to the first Jewish Congress, representing American Jews. At the Congress, he advocated for training people to prepare for bringing Jewish immigrants to Palestine once it was freed from Ottoman rule. His involvement shaped our family’s Zionist commitment and eventual settlement in that land.

explicit_text: Palestine was under Ottoman rule when my grandfather decided to settle there to help prepare for Jewish immigration. The dream was to bring poor Jewish people from the United States to Palestine once it was freed from the Ottoman government. This historical context was crucial for my family’s early involvement in agronomy and settlement work in the region. The transition from Ottoman control set the stage for the Zionist movement that shaped our lives.

This historical context was crucial for my family’s early involvement in agronomy and settlement work in the region. The transition from Ottoman control set the stage for the Zionist movement that shaped our lives.

explicit_text: My grandfather married a German-origin woman who worked at a Bacadus bank in Jaffa. Unfortunately, she became ill with lung problems and had to leave Jaffa to live in Germany. Jaffa was part of my family's early life in Palestine before health issues forced them to relocate. It was a significant place in the early 1900s for my family’s beginnings in the region.
implicit_text: My grandfather married a German-origin woman who worked at a Bacadus bank in the ancient port city of Jaffa. Unfortunately, she became ill with lung problems and had to leave Jaffa to live in Germany. This city was part of my family's early life in Palestine before health issues forced them to relocate. It was a significant place in the early 1900s for my family’s beginnings in the region.

explicit_text: My grandmother was tragically killed in the Theresienstadt camp during the Holocaust. This loss deeply affected my family and is part of our painful history. Despite this, my mother managed to arrive in Israel in 1930, continuing the family’s connection to the land. The memory of Theresienstadt is a somber reminder of the hardships our family endured.

explicit_text: My father lived in Berlin for some time after being sent out from Poznan following World War I. My grandfather also traveled to Berlin for Jewish Congress meetings. Berlin was a significant city in my family’s history during the early 20th century, linking their German origins with their Zionist aspirations. It was a place of both political activity and personal transitions for us.

explicit_text: My father studied at the first Jewish agricultural school located in Ahlem, Hanover. This education was part of his preparation for aliyah and working in agricultural settlement in Israel. Hanover represents an important step in his journey from Germany to Israel around 1930. The school was a foundation for his Zionist commitment and practical skills.

explicit_text: My parents lived and worked in a kibbutz near Kfar Yehezkel and Afula after arriving in Israel. This was part of the early Zionist settlement efforts in the 1930s. The kibbutz experience was central to their lives, contributing to the building of the land and community. It was a place of hard work and hope for the future.

explicit_text: Afula was near the kibbutz where my parents lived and worked after coming to Israel. It was part of the region where early Jewish agricultural settlements were established. This area was important for my family’s involvement in building the land and community in the 1930s. Afula represents the pioneering spirit of those times.

explicit_text: In 1934, my brothers moved to Akko, and my mother worked in a coffee shop there. Akko was part of our family’s life during the early years in Israel. It was a small but growing town where we lived before moving to Nahariya. Akko connects to our story of settlement and community building.

explicit_text: I was born in Nahariya, a town my father helped prepare by draining swamps and developing the land. When Nahariya was founded, my father was given a piece of land as payment for his work. Nahariya has been my lifelong home and the center of my family’s roots in Israel. It symbolizes the hard work and pioneering spirit of my parents’ generation.

explicit_text: One of the children my parents fostered lives in Australia and visits us regularly, spending about six weeks a year in Nahariya. She used to stay with my mother during her vacations and now stays with us. Australia is part of our extended family network, showing how our household has been open and connected to many people over the years. It reflects the ongoing bonds we maintain across continents.

explicit_text: One of the children who grew up in our house recently came from England for a three-week visit with her children. She works as a doctor researching infectious diseases at King's College London. England is part of our family’s extended reach, showing how those we fostered have grown and spread out internationally. These visits keep our family ties alive and vibrant.

explicit_text: After finishing my army service, I traveled extensively, including a trip to India in a Volkswagen Type 2 van that I bought in Germany. Cooking in the van during my travels through India and other countries helped me discover the social power of food. India was a place where I learned how food can bring people together, even in the most challenging environments. This journey was formative in shaping my relationship with food.

explicit_text: During my travels in the Volkswagen van, I passed through Persia, where food was often scarce and the environment harsh. Cooking there and sharing food with locals and travelers taught me about the communal and social aspects of food. Persia was one of the many countries on my route that deepened my appreciation for food as a connector of people. These experiences influenced my culinary perspective.

explicit_text: I bought a Volkswagen Type 2 van in Germany and used it to travel extensively from age 21 to 25, including trips through India, Persia, Afghanistan, and Turkey. The van was my home and kitchen, where I cooked meals that helped me connect with people everywhere I went. Cooking in the van revealed to me the social power of food as a way to make friends and build community. This vehicle was central to my formative travels and culinary experiences.

explicit_text: World War I had a direct impact on my family, especially my father’s birthplace Poznan, which shifted from German to Polish control after the war in 1918. The aftermath of the war led to many Germans, including my father’s family, being sent out of Poznan. This historical event influenced my father’s move to Berlin and later his Zionist journey to Israel. World War I shaped the geopolitical realities that affected my family’s migration.
